preFix.title: Carved birch spice spoon
preFix.subtitle: A small shallow green-birch spoon for salt, spice, or loose tea
preFix.excerpt: A short birch spice spoon with a wide shallow bowl, sized for a salt cellar or spice jar. Birch carves cleanly from a small branch section. The project introduces fine detail work at the bowl rim.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the small-treen carving method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the spice-cellar spoon form.

content.text: A spice spoon sits inside a jar or cellar, lifts a small measured amount, and deposits it cleanly. The bowl is wide relative to its depth so that spice or salt falls off rather than sticking. Birch suits this project well: it is straight-grained in small branch sections, carves cleanly from
green
wood with no power tools, and dries food-safe without any treatment that would flavour the spice. The whole piece is 10 cm long; a beginner can complete it in an afternoon.
Wood

Birch is the recommendation: the grain is fine and straight in a small branch, it dries pale and odourless, and a fresh-cut birch branch is one of the easiest green-wood sources to find in the UK. The starting
blank
needs to be 12 cm long and 3.5 cm wide. A straight branch pruning or windfall section is ideal. Sycamore is a close alternative with equally clean carving properties.
Tools
Sloyd knife: for all shaping. The small blank is too small and the cuts too controlled for the carving axe to be useful here.
Hook knife, small: for the bowl hollow. The tight radius suits the small bowl.
Leather strop: strop every fifteen minutes.
Raw linseed oil for finishing.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Splitting and marking the blank

Split the birch branch section in half lengthways. The pith in birch is small but remove it from whichever half you are working; the pith centre dries unevenly and can split the finished spoon. Mark the spoon profile on the flat face with a pencil: a 7 cm handle tapering from 10 mm wide at the tip to 14 mm at the throat, then a 5 cm bowl section 30 mm wide at the rim.
Shaping the handle

Hold the blank in the four-finger grip. Use push cuts along the handle sides to bring the profile to the drawn line. Birch green is soft; each cut removes a visible shaving. Work from the throat toward the handle tip, not from the tip toward the throat, so cuts go with the grain and the blade exits cleanly at the end. The handle cross-section should be slightly oval, comfortable to hold between two fingers.
The back of the bowl

Hold the blank handle-down and work push cuts from the bowl tip toward the throat to dome the back of the bowl. Birch grain in a small branch runs end-to-end; cuts from tip to throat go with the grain and lift cleanly. Use
thumb-pivot cuts
around the rim edge to blend the back of the bowl smoothly into the sides. The spice-spoon bowl back needs only a gentle curve; leave it fairly flat at 4 to 5 mm dome height.
Hollowing the bowl

The small hook knife works the bowl hollow. The target depth is 4 to 5 mm in the centre; the bowl rim is 2 mm thick. On a piece this small the hollowing takes only six to eight strokes per side. Work from the rim toward the centre in short arcs. Strop after every ten strokes; birch loads the strop with pale wood dust and the edge dulls quickly.
Finishing

Air-dry overnight. Apply raw linseed oil with a fingertip, let soak for one hour, wipe off. Repeat once. Allow two weeks to cure before food use. The short drying time reflects the small piece, not a faster cure: linseed cure time is fixed by chemistry, not by piece size.
Variations
Salt-cellar spoon: leave the handle tip rounded for sitting inside a lidded cellar.
Loose-leaf tea spoon: increase the bowl width to 35 mm and shallow the depth to 3 mm for a level teaspoon measure.
Matching set: carve three or four from the same branch for a matched condiment-spoon set.
Care
Rinse with cold water; never hot soak, never dishwasher. Dry thoroughly before returning to a closed spice jar. Re-oil once a year or when the wood looks dry.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Keep rims at least 2 mm; 1.5 mm is the threshold below which thin green-birch bowls regularly crack as they dry. A small crack can be pressed together with a warm wipe of linseed.
attrs.cause: Rim left too thin in green wood
attrs.symptom: The bowl rim cracked during drying

attrs.fix: Ensure the two-week cure time has passed. A very light sanding of the bowl interior with 320-grit before oiling gives a smoother surface that releases spice cleanly.
attrs.cause: Oil not fully cured or surface too rough
attrs.symptom: Spice sticks to the bowl surface

attrs.fix: A spice spoon handle should be 10 to 12 mm wide at the narrowest. If it is thinner, add a wrapping of thin linen thread around the grip section, fixed with a drop of raw linseed.
attrs.symptom: The handle feels too thin to grip

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
brand-trademark: brand name "Target" — generic equivalent is "(any general-purpose shop)"
